author: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

title: Chapter 23

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 23.—51.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "But if you say that we give baptism twice over, truly it is rather you who do this, who slay men who have been baptized; and this we do not say because you baptize them, but because you cause each one of them, by the act of slaying him, to be baptized in his own blood.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For the baptism of water or of the Spirit is as it were doubled when the blood of the martyr is wrung from him.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And so our Saviour also Himself, after being baptized in the first instance by John, declared that He must be baptized again, not this time with water nor with the Spirit, but with the baptism of blood, the cross of suffering, as it is written, ‘Two disciples, the sons of Zebedee, came unto Him, saying, Lord, when thou comest into thy kingdom grant that we may sit, one on Thy right hand, and the other on Thy left hand.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But Jesus said unto them, Ye ask a difficult thing: can ye drink of the cup that I drink of, and be baptized with the baptism that I am baptized with?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: They said unto Him, We are able.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And He said unto them, Ye can indeed drink of the cup that I drink of; and with the baptism that I am baptized withal shall ye be baptized,’ and so forth.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If these are two baptisms, you commend us by your malice, we must needs confess.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For when you kill our bodies, then we do celebrate a second baptism; but it is that we are baptized with our baptism and with blood, like Christ.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Blush, blush, ye persecutors.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Ye make martyrs like unto Christ, who are sprinkled with the baptism of blood after the water of the genuine baptism."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: In the first place, we reply without delay that we do not kill you, but you kill yourselves by a true death, when you cut yourselves off from the living root of unity.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: In the next place, if all who are killed are baptized in their own blood, then all robbers, all unrighteous, impious, accursed men, who are put to death by the sentence of the law, are to be considered martyrs, because they are baptized in their own blood.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if only those are baptized in their own blood who are put to death for righteousness’ sake, since theirs is the kingdom of heaven, you have already seen that the first question is why you suffer, and only afterwards should we ask what you suffer.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why therefore do you puff out your cheeks before you have shown the righteousness of your deeds?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why, does your tongue resound before your character is approved?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If you have made a schism, you are impious; if you are impious, you die as one guilty of sacrilege, when you are punished for impiety; if you die as one guilty of sacrilege, how are you baptized in your blood?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Or do you say, I have not made a schism?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let us then inquire into this.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why do you make an outcry before you prove your case?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Or do you say, Even if I am guilty of sacrilege, I ought not to be slain by you?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: It is one question as to the enormity of my action, which you never prove with any truth, another as to the baptism of your blood, from whence you derive your boast.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For I never killed you, nor do you prove that you are killed by any one.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Nor even if you were to prove it would it in any way affect me, whoever it was that killed you, whether he did it justly in virtue of power lawfully given by the Lord, or committed the crime of murder, like the chaff of the Lord’s harvest, through some evil desire; just as you are in no way concerned with him who in recent times, with an intolerable tyranny, attended even by a company of soldiers, not because he feared any one, but that he might be feared by all, oppressed widows, destroyed pupils, betrayed the patrimonies of other men, annulled the marriages of other men, contrived the sale of the property of the innocent, divided the price of the property when sold with its mourning owners.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I should seem to be saying all this out of the invention of my own head, if it were not sufficiently obvious of whom I speak without the mention of his name.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And if all this is undoubtedly true, then just as you are not concerned with this, so neither are we concerned with anything you say, even though it were true.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if that colleague of yours, being really a just and innocent man, is maligned by a lying tale, then should we also learn in no way to give credit to reports, which have been spread abroad of innocent men, as though they had delivered up the sacred books, or murdered any of their fellow-men.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: To this we may add, that I refer to a man who lived with you, whose birthday you were wont to celebrate with such large assemblies, with whom you joined in the kiss of peace in the sacraments, in whose hands you placed the Eucharist, to whom in turn you extended your hands to receive it from his ministering, whose ears, when they were deaf amid the groanings of all Africa, you durst not offend by free speech; for paying to whom, even indirectly, a most witty compliment, by saying that in the Count he had a god for his companion, some one of your party was extolled to the skies.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But you reproach us with the deeds of men with whom we never lived, whose faces we never saw, in whose lifetime we were either boys, or perhaps as yet not even born.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What is the meaning, then, of your great unfairness and perversity, that you should wish to impose on us the burdens of those whom we never knew, whilst you will not bear the burdens of your friends?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: The divine Scriptures exclaim: "When thou sawest a thief, then thou consentedst with him."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If he whom you saw did not pollute you, why do you reproach me with one whom I could not have seen?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Or do you say, I did not consent with him, because his deeds were displeasing to me?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But, at any rate, you went up to the altar of God with him.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Come now, if you would defend yourself, make a distinction between your two positions, and say that it is one thing to consent together for sin, as the two elders consented together when they laid a plot against the chastity of Susannah, and another thing to receive the sacrament of the Lord in company with a thief, as the apostles received even that first supper in company with Judas.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I am all in favor of your defense.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But why do you not consider how much more easily, in the course of your defense, you have acquitted all the nations and boundaries of the earth, throughout which the inheritance of Christ is dispersed?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if it was possible for you to see a thief, and to share the sacraments with the thief whom you saw, and yet not to share his sin, how much less was it possible for the remotest nations of the earth to have anything in common with the sins of African traditors and persecutors, supposing your charges and assertions to be true, even though they held the sacraments in common with them?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Or do you say, I saw in him the bishop, I did not see in him the thief?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Say what you will.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I allow this defense also, and in this the world is acquitted of the charges which you brought against it.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if it was permitted you to ignore the character of a man whom you knew, why is the whole world not allowed to be ignorant of those it never knew, unless, indeed, the Donatists are allowed to be ignorant of what they do not wish to know, while the nations of the earth may not be ignorant of what they cannot know?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Or do you say, Theft is one thing, delivery of the sacred books or persecution is another?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I grant there is a difference, nor is it worth while now to show wherein that difference consists.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But listen to the summary of the argument.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If he could not make you a thief, because his thieving was displeasing in your sight, who can make men traditors or murderers to whom such treachery or murder is abhorrent?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: First, then, confess that you share in all the evil of Optatus, whom you knew, and even so reproach me with any evil which was found in those whom I knew not.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And do not say to me, But my charges are serious, yours but trifling.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You must first acknowledge them, however trifling they may be in your case, not before I on my side confess the charges against me, but before I can allow you to say these serious things about me at all.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Did Optatus, whom you knew make you a thief by being your colleague, or not?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Answer me one or the other.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If you say he did not, I ask why he did not,—because he was not a thief himself? or because you do not know it? or because you disapprove of it?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If you say, Because he himself was not a thief, much more ought we not to believe that those with whom you reproach us were of such a character as you assert.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if we must not believe of Optatus what both Christians and pagans and Jews, ay, and what both our party and yours assert, how much less should we believe what you assert of any one?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if you say, Because you do not know it, all the nations of the earth answer you, Much more do we not know of all that you reproach us with in these men.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if you say, Because you disapproved of it, they answer you with the same voice, Although you have never proved the truth of what you say, yet acts like these are viewed by us with disapproval.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if you say, Lo, Optatus, whom I knew, made me a thief because he was my colleague, and I was in the habit of going to the altar with him when he committed those deeds; but I do not greatly heed it, because the fault was trivial, but your party made you a traditor and a murderer,—I answer that I do not allow that I too am made a traditor and a murderer by the sins of other men, just because you confess that you are made a thief by the sin of another man; for it must be remembered that you are proved a thief, not by our judgment, but by your own confession.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For we say that every man must bear his own burden, as the apostle is our witness.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But you, of your own accord, have taken the burden of Optatus on your own shoulders, not because you committed the theft, or consented to it, but because you declared your conviction that what another did applied to you.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For, as the apostle says, when speaking of food, "I know, and am persuaded by the Lord Jesus, that there is nothing unclean of itself: but to him that esteemeth anything to be unclean, to him it is unclean;" by the same rule, it may be said that the sins of others cannot implicate those who disapprove of them; but if any one thinks that they affect him, then he is affected by them.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Wherefore you do not convict us of being traditors or murderers, even though you were to prove something of the sort against those who share the sacraments with us; but the guilt of theft is fastened on you, even if you disapprove of everything that Optatus did, not in virtue of our accusation, but by your own decision.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And that you may not think this a trivial fault, read what the apostle says, "Nor shall thieves inherit the kingdom of God."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But those who shall not inherit the kingdom of God will certainly not be on His right hand among those whom it shall be said, "Come, ye blessed of my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If they are not there, where will they be except on the left hand?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Therefore among those to whom it shall be said, "Depart from me, ye cursed, into everlasting fire, prepared for the devil and his angels."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: In vain, therefore, do you indulge in your security, thinking it a trivial fault which separates you from the kingdom of God, and sends you into everlasting fire.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: How much better will you do to betake yourself to true confusion, saying, Every one of us shall bear his own burden, and the winnowing fan at the last day shall separate the chaff from the wheat!

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But it is evident that you are afraid of its being forthwith said to you, "Why then, whilst you attempt to place on some men’s backs the burdens of their neighbors, have you dared to separate yourselves from the Lord’s corn, dispersed throughout the world, before the winnowing at the last day?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Accordingly, you who disapprove of the deeds of your party, whilst you are taking precautions against being charged with the schism which you all have made, are involving yourselves also in their sins which you did not commit; and while the shrewd Petilianus is afraid of my being able to say that am I not such as he thinks Cæcilianus was, he is obliged to confess that he himself is such as he knows Optatus to have been.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Or are you not such as the common voice of Africa proclaims him to have been?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Then neither are we such as those with whom you reproach us are either suspected to have been by your mistake, or calumniously asserted to have been by your madness, or proved to have been by the truth.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Much less is the wheat of the Lord in all the nations of the earth of such a character, seeing that it never heard the names of those of whom you speak.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: There is therefore no reason why you should perish in such sin of separation and such sacrilege of schism.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And yet, if you are made to suffer for this great impiety by the judgment of God, you say that you are even baptized in your blood; so that you are not content with feeling no remorse for your division, but you must even glory in your punishment.
